editableNewDialogue.text: I’ve heard people say the brain “replays” things during sleep. Is that literally true, or just a metaphor?

It’s partly literal. During sleep—especially deep NREM sleep—patterns of neural activity linked to recent experiences can be reactivated (“replay”), which is one mechanism believed to support memory consolidation. It’s not like a perfect video replay, but measurable sequences of firing can recur in similar order, particularly in the hippocampus and related networks.
Does REM do the same thing, or is it different?

Different emphasis. REM sleep tends to show more associative, emotionally tinted processing—integrating memories, extracting themes, and linking distant ideas. NREM replay is often described as more “stabilizing” and “indexing” memories, while REM may help with emotional recalibration and creative recombination. Both stages interact across the night.
Random question: why do some people wake up right before an alarm, like their body predicts it?

A common explanation is circadian timing plus expectation. If you regularly wake around a certain time (or you set an alarm and anticipate it), your brain can increase arousal signals ahead of that time. Hormones like cortisol also rise naturally toward morning, and lighter sleep becomes more common, making spontaneous waking more likely.
I read that octopuses might dream—real or hype?

It’s plausible but not proven in the human sense. Octopuses show sleep-like states, including a “quiet” phase and an “active” phase where their skin patterning rapidly changes and their eyes/body may twitch. Some researchers think the active phase could involve memory processing or dream-like internal activity, but we can’t confirm subjective dreaming without behavioral or neural markers that map clearly to it.
One last thing—what’s a simple way to remember the difference between circadian rhythm and sleep pressure?

Circadian rhythm is the clock (time-of-day drive), anchored by light and coordinated by the brain’s master clock. Sleep pressure is the hourglass (time-awake drive), building the longer you’re awake due to factors like adenosine accumulation and easing after sleep.
If I try to start again, I don’t even have a piano at home now, so I’d probably need to go somewhere and rent one for an hour to see what my fingers remember.

That’s a smart way to test it—one short session on a rented instrument can show you what’s still there (often more than you expect), and what needs rebuilding. If you tell me what you used to play (classical pieces, chords/songs, improvising), I can suggest a simple “first hour back” plan.
About that thing I said earlier—I actually don’t read before bed anymore. I stopped because it keeps my brain too active.

Got it—so reading before bed used to be your routine, but now you avoid it because it makes it harder to wind down. When you do want something before sleep, is it more like quiet music or just lights out?
Yeah, exactly. I used to, but not now. I just try to sleep without reading.
